item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Solid concept and mostly good execution, but the tone and excesses put me off.

1- The excess technobable and Foundation-verse specifics get a lot of focus out of how alien Alexylva is supposed to feel. There is no reason for an MTF being involved, and specially the Foundation having a grasp and a name for exactly how Alexylva created this takes them from alien to bland. They naming Alexylva directly in the description doesn't help it, either, specially done as if this was enough to explain something;

2- I feel that a lot here is creative laziness. A lengthy containment that sums up to "lock it up and build a wall", two instances of "at the atomic level" without a grasp of what it really means, use of shocking comments instead of actual tests, unnecessary details on the D-Class for an exploration that was bland and were his individuality ended up having no bearing.

Overall the final documents are ok, not bad, not good either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My one issue is that it never specifies why the foundation haven't been able to prevent this effect by going after the precise hume generators that it knows exist. It assumes knowledge of Alexylva, as far as I can see.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded:
ResearcherLi, staff has received complaints about your immature behavior, specifically repeatedly posting low-content spammy comments.

Please endeavor to improve.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *cough*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He was reassigned to 110-Montauk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
?

i don't get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nhhh. This doesn't feel like Alexylva; the tone of the documentation feels really off, and the "humane death is not a priority" note seems like a shallow attempt to display the off-kilter morality in their universe. Plus the whole "Hail Caesar" thing really does not jive with my headcanon of their world being Phoenician instead of purely Greco-Roman.

No-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not a bad idea, and I like the re-emergence of Alexylva, but the body horror isn't properly—viscerally—explored, the description up until the exploration log is not terribly interesting and seems arbitrary in places (can't Foundation employees use high-powered cutting devices to escape rather than having to die?), and the final notes suddenly and jarringly explain everything. This article suffers from telling rather than showing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since they figured out how easy it is to rig a trial to convict someone on bullshit charges, or give them hella sentences for legitimate minor charges, if you really want them in jail. The government won't cop to it and media outlets don't call it that, but that doesn't mean it doesn't happen.

A less contentious explanation might be that this prisoner learned a dialect of English that uses "y'all", either natively (before moving to another country and being taken prisoner there), or just as a second language. Some people deliberately incorporate "y'all" into their vocabulary, as well.

That said, I don't really buy the Foundation routinely taking political prisoners as D-Class. People with a proven penchant for fighting the power do not make ideal pliant test subjects, and are generally watched closely by activist groups.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The political prisoner D class has a"y'all" in his vocabulary? Since when did America start keeping political prisoners from in States?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is leaving me more with a blank "I don't get it" impression than of one of curiosity of the mystery behind all this. If this thing is supposed to be a humane execution chamber, how was it supposed to work as one until things went wrong?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. Interesting, but needs to be fleshed out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one actually disturbed me a lot. The thought of being trapped in a wall concious and aware but unable to move or die is extremely unsettling to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The body horror is never convincingly realised or shocking enough to have impact, and the exploration log doesn't add anything in particular to our knowledge of the skip. As a result, the whole story is contained in the last few Alexylva notes, which come across as blandly expository. This one is not grabbing me.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Image has been edited out until a compliant source can be found.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded:
Picture #1 is my own, taken near my home.

Picture #2: http://visual-records.wdfiles.com/local--files/humanoid/tumblr_lyxw5kWweE1r4rj25o1_400.jpg Source table on visual records wiki is bugged so I'll refrain from deleting until fixed

item.title: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM and will have 48 hours to properly source the image before it is edited out of the page. If the images are not wholly your own, please post the image's source as a link (or links) in reply to this post and edit it into your first post in this discussion thread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, buddy, that's what, two Alexylva skips in as many months? I'm all for this revival. :D

item.title: Done.
item.content:encoded:
Thanks, everyone. I didn't see the pointlessness of the primer-doc until now, but it's gone.

Sources:

Picture #1 is my own, taken near my home.

Picture #2: http://visual-records.wdfiles.com/local--files/humanoid/tumblr_lyxw5kWweE1r4rj25o1_400.jpg Source table on visual records wiki is bugged so I'll refrain from deleting until fixed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The obfuscation document serves no purpose, this is not an infohazard. I would suggest simply deleting it and you'll have a solid skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is beautiful, very well done. I saw a grammar error here and there but I can't do a full review at the moment, since I'm in class. As soon as I get home, I'll find all the little nitpickings and send them to you.

+1, the exploration logs really sealed the deal for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fake-out documentation that serves no real purpose, and lumping all the story in some convenient end-notes make something that could have been satisfying rote and uninspired. I would focus on making the exploration more vivid and substantial, drop the useless primer-doc, and spread some of this Alvexia info throughout instead of unceremoniously dumping it at the end to compensate for the weak narrative preceding it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please delete your tags until the tag team fills them in for you, some of these aren't even tags, like school.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: haven't seen an alexylva SCP in a while, pretty cool! +1
